narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the admissions model predicted a 45% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for Year 1 grades at your school, the second-lowest for Year 3, a low family income bracket, an LSAT score of 31.5, an undergraduate GPA of 2.9, identifying as female and Hispanic, and planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students, who average around the middle of their class in those early deciles (about 5th or 6th out of 10), much higher LSAT scores near 37, GPAs over 3.2, and mostly higher family incomes, your academic markers stand out as notably below average. The model weighed these factors to assess your bar exam odds, with your Year 3 class rank emerging as the single most influential element, strongly nudging the prediction toward failure because it was so low relative to peers. Next in line was your LSAT score, which also pushed the outlook toward failure by falling well short of the norm. Your Year 1 rank came third, similarly contributing to concerns due to its bottom-decile position. Low family income ranked fourth, adding to the risk signal in the model's eyes, followed by your Hispanic background in fifth place. Your undergraduate GPA placed sixth, reinforcing the pattern with its below-average level, while full-time status and gender had the least sway, still slightly tilting toward failure but minimally so. Overall, no factors pulled strongly in the positive direction for you, creating a consensus toward elevated failure risk. This ranking reflects how much each trait shifted the prediction for your unique situation away from the typical successful path. To improve your chances next time, focus on bolstering academic performance—consider targeted prep for retaking the LSAT if possible, building stronger study habits to climb those class deciles in future coursework, and seeking scholarships or support programs tailored to students from low-income or underrepresented backgrounds like yours. With dedication to these areas, you could shift your profile closer to the averages that predict bar success. Keep pushing forward; many students overcome similar hurdles.
